coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen, sulfur, and phosphorus Co-doped carbon dots-based ratiometric chemosensor for highly selective sequential detection of Al3+ and Fe3+ ions in logic gate, cell imaging, and real sample analysis

Palanisamy, Subramanian
You, SangGuan

Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: Heteroatom-doped photoluminescent (PL) carbon dots (CDs) have recently gained attention as optical sensors due to their excellent tunable properties. In this work, we propose a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of PL nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P) co-doped carbon dots (NSP-CDs) using glutathione and phosphoric acid (H3PO4) as precursors. The synthesized NSP-CDs were characterized using different spectroscopic and microscopic techniques, including ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The NSP-CDs exhibited excellent PL properties with green emission at 492 nm upon excitation at 417 nm, a high quantum yield of 26.7%, and dependent emission behavior. The as-prepared NSP-CDs were spherical with a well-monodispersed average particle size of 5.2 nm. Moreover, NSP-CDs demonstrate high PL stability toward a wider pH, high salt ionic strength, and various solvents. Furthermore, the NSP-CDs showed a three-state “off–on–off” PL response upon the sequential addition of Al3+ and Fe3+ ions, with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 10.8 nM for Al3+ and 50.7 nM for Fe3+. The NSP-CD sensor can construct an INHIBIT logic gate with Al3+ and Fe3+ ions as the chemical inputs and emissions as the output mode. Owing to an excellent tunable PL property and biocompatibility, the NSP-CDs were applied for sensing Al3+ and Fe3+ ions as well as live cell imaging. Furthermore, NSP-CDs were designed as PL sensors for detecting Al3+ and Fe3+ ions in real water show their potential application.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heteroatom-doped photoluminescent (PL) carbon dots (CDs) have recently gained attention as optical sensors due to their excellent tunable properties. In this work, we propose a one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of PL nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P) co-doped carbon dots (NSP-CDs) using glutathione and phosphoric acid (H3PO4) as precursors. The synthesized NSP-CDs were characterized using different spectroscopic and microscopic techniques, including ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The NSP-CDs exhibited excellent PL properties with green emission at 492 nm upon excitation at 417 nm, a high quantum yield of 26.7%, and dependent emission behavior. The as-prepared NSP-CDs were spherical with a well-monodispersed average particle size of 5.2 nm. Moreover, NSP-CDs demonstrate high PL stability toward a wider pH, high salt ionic strength, and various solvents. Furthermore, the NSP-CDs showed a three-state “off–on–off” PL response upon the sequential addition of Al3+ and Fe3+ ions, with a low limit of detection (LOD) of 10.8 nM for Al3+ and 50.7 nM for Fe3+. The NSP-CD sensor can construct an INHIBIT logic gate with Al3+ and Fe3+ ions as the chemical inputs and emissions as the output mode. Owing to an excellent tunable PL property and biocompatibility, the NSP-CDs were applied for sensing Al3+ and Fe3+ ions as well as live cell imaging. Furthermore, NSP-CDs were designed as PL sensors for detecting Al3+ and Fe3+ ions in real water show their potential application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have attracted considerable attention owing to their small size, photostability, low toxicity, high water solubility, biocompatibility, and ease of surface functionalization (Behi et al., 2022). Recently, they have become the focus of interest owing to their potential application as chemosensors, biosensors, cell imaging, and catalysts (Magesh et al., 2022). CDs have several advantages over traditional quantum dots and organic fluorescent compounds, including minimal toxicity, powerful photoluminescence, and excellent chemical stability (Omar et al., 2022). CDs can be synthesized using thermal decomposition, solvothermal, hydrothermal, microwave, laser ablation, ultrasonic, arc-discharge, and electrochemical methods (Kaur et al., 2022). Among these methods, the hydrothermal approach is advantageous because it is inexpensive, nontoxic, and environmentally benign (Ullal et al., 2022). Additionally, heteroatom doping can enhance the optical characteristics of CDs and thus provide a high quantum yield. CDs have been doped with a variety of elements, including N, B, S, and P to obtain excitation-dependent or independent photoluminescence characteristics via the passivation of surface states and defects (Chandra et al., 2016; Dong et al., 2020; Kalaiyarasan et al., 2020; Li and Xu, 2020; Molkenova and Atabaev, 2019; Wang et al., 2022, 2021; Wang et al., 2019). The heteroatom doping might modify electronic characteristics and thus introduce more active moieties, regulate optical features, and customize surface defects into CDs. Therefore, heteroatom-doped CDs have been developed as diverse chemosensors with intriguing capabilities in biomaterial sensing and living cell imaging (Xu et al., 2016).

The most frequently employed doping elements are N, S, and P, which have strong binding properties and atomic sizes similar to carbon (C) and can alter the optical performance or function of CDs. For instance, P-CDs were created from dextrose by Molkenova and Atabaev (2019) to detect low concentrations of metal ions in aqueous media. Kalaiyarasan et al. (2020) developed P-doped CQDs as fluorometric probes for metal-ion detection. Wang et al. (2019) reported P and Cl co-doped CDs as fluorescent probes with high photoluminescence for metal-ion sensing. Chandra et al. (2016) investigated highly fluorescent N- and P-doped CDs to sense metal ions in cancer cells. N, P-doped carbon dots (NP-CDs) were easily produced from maize starch by Dong et al. (2020) using a solvothermal method for highly sensitive detection. Wang et al. (2022) created highly luminous nucleoside-based N, P-doped CDs for bioimaging and sensitive metal ion detection. Importantly, NSP-CDs prepared by Li and Xu (2020) and Wang et al. (2021) exhibit strong fluorescence and thus can be employed for photocatalytic and metal-ion sensing applications. As a result, the synergistic effect is crucial in utilizing nanomaterials, and doping CDs with various heteroatoms can help adjust their properties. To the best of our knowledge, there are limited reports of NSP-CDs with high quantum yield (QY) synthesized from affordable and sustainable raw materials, and their superior PL features are unusual. Consequently, it is still essential to produce novel, highly photoluminescent (PL) CDs for detection using a straightforward synthesis method and inexpensive precursors.

Herein, we report a green, economical, and simple method for synthesizing NSP-CDs using a one-pot hydrothermal process with glutathione and H3PO4 as precursors. The as-prepared NSP-CDs exhibited low cytotoxicity, excellent water solubility, a high PL QY, and green emission. The structure, morphology, and optical properties of NSP-CDs were characterized. Using an aqueous medium, the NSP-CDs displayed a highly selective and sensitive response to Al3+ and sequential sensing with a quenching response to Fe3+ ions (Scheme S1). Furthermore, NSP-CDs were used to sensing Al3+ and Fe3+ ions in living cells, showing their potential application in cell imaging. The advantages of our proposed PL probe were confirmed using tap and lake water samples. Accordingly, a photoluminescence assay was developed for detecting Al3+ and Fe3+ ions at very low concentrations.

Glutathione and H3PO4 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All the metal nitrates and chloride salts were purchased from Merck. All compounds were of analytical grade and were used for synthesis without further purification. Milli-Q water was used to prepare all the solutions for the entire experiment.

NSP-CDs were prepared using glutathione and H3PO4 as precursors via a one-pot hydrothermal process based on a reported method (Li and Xu, 2020; Wang et al., 2021). Briefly, 0.1 g glutathione was dissolved in 30 mL of Milli-Q water, which was then mixed with 3.0 mL H3PO4 to synthesize the NSP-CDs. The prepared mixture was placed in a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated for 12 h at 180 °C. The autoclave was cooled to room temperature after the reaction, and a slightly yellowish solution of NSP-CDs was obtained. The large particles of the resulting solution were removed using a 0.22 μm pore size membrane. Then, the yellowish solution was filtered for 24 h using a dialysis bag (1000 Da). Finally, NSP-CDs were obtained as a solid, which was dried in an oven for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Al3+ and Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The PL spectra were measured to study the selectivity and sensitivity of NSP-CDs. The metal ions (Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Sr2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Na+, Ir3+, Ni2+, and Zn2+) were detected in Milli-Q water. Appropriate volumes of an aqueous solution of 25 μL metal ions (concentration ranges up to 5.0 μM) and the dispersion of 5 μL NSP-CDs (10 μg/mL) were mixed with 1.970 mL Milli-Q water. To further validate the sensitivity effect of Al3+ concentrations (0–5.0 μM) added to 10 μg/mL NSP-CDs, the sequential detection of Fe3+ at various concentrations (0–10 μM) was performed. The PL emission enhancement [1/(P–P0)] and quenching efficiency (P0/P) were calculated, where P0 is the PL intensity of the blank solution (absence Al3+ and Fe3+ ions), and P is the PL intensity of the solutions (with Al3+ and Fe3+ ions). Finally, the PL spectra were obtained between 425 and 650 nm with 417 nm excitation.

A human colorectal cancer cell line (HCT-116) was purchased from the Korean Cell Line Bank (Seoul, South Korea) and kept alive in fetal bovine serum (FBS), DMEM with 10% (v/v), and 1% antibiotic solution at room temperature (RT) in an incubate with 5% CO2. The cytotoxicity of NSP-CDs was studied in HCT-116 cell lines using the standard WST assay. HCT-116 cells (1 × 105) per well were scattered into a 96-well plate for 24 h and then treated with different concentrations of tested NSP-CDs from 0 to 150 μg/mL at RT for another 24 h. Finally, during the incubation period, the WST solution (50 μL, 5 mg/mL) was mixed with all samples for 6 h of incubation at 570 nm absorbance measured using a Bio-Rad microplate reader. NSP-CDs, NSP-CDs/Al3+, and NSP-CDs/Al3+ + Fe3+-tagged HCT-116 cells were detected by fluorescence microscopy (Olympus FV1200, Japan). Three tests were performed and expressed as the mean ± standard deviation.

The optical properties of the NSP-CDs were measured by UV–Vis absorption and PL spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 1 . In Fig. 1a, three major absorption bands at 318, 270, and 228 nm, which correspond to CO bonds (n-π* transition) and CC, NP or CN bonds (π–π* transition) were obtained in the UV–vis spectra of NSP-CDs (Li and Xu, 2020; Wang et al., 2021). Under daylight, the NSP-CD solution was light yellow and clear. In contrast, it produced a bright green color when exposed to UV lamp irradiation at 365 nm (inset in Fig. 1a). Additionally, the NSP-CDs exhibited the highest excitation and emission intensity with maxima at 417 and 492 nm, respectively. The PL characteristics of NSP-CDs excitation variation with emission intensity were also investigated as shown in Fig. 1b. The PL peak of the NSP-CDs regularly redshifted (from 480 to 544 nm) as the excitation wavelength varied from 390 to 500 nm with a constant increment of 10 nm (Omar et al., 2022). This excitation-dependent behavior might be related to the various sizes of the NSP-CD particles and the distribution of surface states (Ding et al., 2020).

Our preliminary study assessed the optical PL stability of NSP-CDs in various environments, including pH, salt media, and solvents treated with UV radiation. The prepared NSP-CDs showed robust and stable PL intensity from pH 1.0 to 7.0 (Li and Zhang, 2014). However, the PL intensity considerably decreased from pH 9.0 to 13.0 (Ma et al., 2020) (Fig. S1a), indicating it was pH-dependent. The numerous acidic or basic functional groups, including –COOH, –SH, –NH, –NH2, and –OH present on the surface of the NSP-CDs may be the reason for their pH-dependent PL characteristics (Naik et al., 2022). This outcome could be the result of these groups being protonated or deprotonated depending on the pH levels in the region, which changed the surface characteristics of NSP-CDs and affected the rate at which electron-hole pairs nonradiative recombined, changing the PL intensity. The effect of interference of 0–1.0 M NaCl on the PL intensity of the NSP-CDs is shown in Fig. S1b. It is evident that even at 1.0 M, the NaCl concentration did not affect the PL intensity, proving the viability of using NSP-CDs under physiological salt conditions or seawater (Qin et al., 2021). The effect of solvents, including acetonitrile, 1,4-Dioxane, dimethylformamide, ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide, methanol, ethyl acetate, tetrahydrofuran, and deionized water, on the PL emission properties of the NSP-CDs was examined. Fig. S1c shows the typical solvatochromic characteristics of the NSP-CDs caused by the interaction between the surface functional groups of NSP-CDs and the solvents (Basu and Mandal, 2018; Chao et al., 2018; Huo et al., 2021). The corresponding PL intensity changes in the pH, salt medium, and solvents were optimized visibly under UV light irradiation, as shown in Figs. S1d−S1f. Owing to the exceptional PL properties and abundant functional groups, the prepared NSP-CDs were used in a sensing experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface and morphological properties of NSP-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface and morphological characteristics of the NSP-CDs were investigated using FT-IR, XRD, TEM, and XPS analysis. The FTIR spectra of the NSP-CDs were used to characterize the functional groups on their surface (Fig. 2 a). Sharp absorption peaks at 3383 and 3187 cm−1 established the presence of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations on the NSP-CD surface (Tripathi et al., 2020). Simultaneously, the peaks at 2819, 1661, and 1098 cm−1 designate the N–H bending vibration and C–H, CO/CC, and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively. Moreover, the typical peaks at 1043 and 640 cm−1 suggest the existence of P–O/C–O–C and S =O/P =O stretching vibrations, respectively (Gao et al., 2020). As illustrated above, the prepared CDs contain C, N, O, S, and P. The typical XRD pattern in Fig. 2b exhibits two prominent peaks (2θ) at 21.2° and 42.1°, which are assigned to the (002) and (101) crystal planes of graphitic carbon, respectively, demonstrating the amorphous nature of the NSP-CDs (Tammina et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2018). In the TEM image, the NSP-CDs have a roughly spherical particle shape and a relatively uniform dispersion (Fig. 2c). The particle size distribution plot shows that the typical particle size varies between 3 and 8 nm, and the average particle size is around 5.2 nm (Fig. 2d) (Gao et al., 2020; Li and Xu, 2020; Wang et al., 2021).

The elemental composition of the NSP-CDs was examined using the XPS technique. Fig. 3 a shows the XPS full-scan profile of the NSP-CDs. According to the analysis findings, N, S, P, C, and O exhibited distinct peaks in the spectrum. Five prominent peaks are observed at 401.5, 169.4, 135.1, 285.3, and 532.6 eV corresponding to the binding energy of N1s, S2p, P2p, C1s, and O1s, respectively (Gao et al., 2020; Li and Xu, 2020; Tripathi et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). The atomic percentages of N, S, P, C, and O were 11.69%, 10.66%, 20.64%, 38.19%, and 18.82%, respectively, confirming the successful doping of P into the NSP-CDs. The high-resolution N1s spectrum of NSP-CDs shows five peaks at 395.6, 397.7, 400.8, 401.6, and 402.5 eV consistent with the binding energies of N–O, pyridinic N, N–CO, graphitic N, and N–C bonds, respectively (Fig. 3b) (Li and Xu, 2020). The S2p spectra exposed five major de-convoluted peaks at 169.0 eV (S–O), 169.8 eV (S2p3/2), and 170.1 eV (S2p1/2) (Fig. 3c) (Wang et al., 2021). The P2p spectrum shows two peaks centered at 133.9, 135.1, and 135.7 eV, indicating that P exists as P2p3/2, P2p1/2, and P–O, respectively (Fig. 3d) (Wang et al., 2021). The C1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.7, 286.4, and 289.1 eV assigned to the aromatic carbon of C–C/C–H, C–O/C–P, and OC–O, respectively (Fig. 3e) (Tripathi et al., 2020). The O1s spectra exhibited three peaks at 531.6 eV (CO), 532.8 eV (C–OH), and 533.8 eV (C–O–C) (Fig. 3f) (Tripathi et al., 2020). These results indicate that –NH2, –SH, –OH, and –COOH groups were formed on the surface of the prepared NSP-CDs, which is consistent with the FTIR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NSP-CDs as a photoluminescent probe for Al3+
ce:section.ce:para: The NSP-CDs were used as PL sensors because of their superior PL properties and high water stability. The selectivity and sensitivity of NSP-CDs toward different metal ions at a uniform concentration of 5.0 μM were first examined using the PL response (Fig. 4 ). According to the experimental results, only Al3+ ions exhibit a noticeable shift in the PL intensity ratio. After the addition of 5.0 μM of Al3+ ions, an emission wavelength of 469 nm from 492 nm (λex∼417 nm) was observed for the NSP-CDs, significantly increasing the PL intensities and causing a considerable blue shift (Fig. 4a) (Hassan et al., 2021). NSP-CDs demonstrate specific recognition of individual metal ions owing to their unique structure and the surface state of the heteroatom-doped NSP-CDs (Chandra et al., 2016; Dong et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022, 2021).

However, the addition of other tested metal ions, including Ag+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Pb2+, Sn2+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Sr2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Na+, Ir3+, Ni2+, and Zn2+ showed very little effect on the NSP-CD PL intensities, as observed in Fig. 4b. As shown in Fig. 4c, the NSP-CD produced strong green photoluminescence. The PL of the NSP-CDs varied from green to blue emission upon adding Al3+ ions under UV light. Al3+ ions induced the most significant PL change, while other metal ions showed almost no effect on the photoluminescence of the chemosensor NSP-CDs. Therefore, compared to remaining cations examined under the same conditions, the chemosensor NSP-CDs exhibited a stronger selectivity for Al3+. These findings imply that the NSP-CDs can be utilized as Al3+-selective PL chemosensors.

The sensitivity of NSP-CDs to Al3+ ions was further observed in the PL spectra. Free NSP-CDs displayed a single emission band centered at 492 nm. The progressive addition of Al3+ ions to the NSP-CDs resulted in a gradual increase in the PL signal at 469 nm and a remarkable decrease at 492 nm (Fig. 5 a) (Li et al., 2013). These results confirmed the ratiometric PL spectra and isosbestic point at 484 nm when the concentration of Al3+ ions increased from 0 to 5.0 μM (Roy et al., 2016). As shown by Job's plot at 469 nm, the NSP-CD chemosensor formed a 1:1 complex (Fig. 5b). Moreover, the PL enhancement of the NSP-CDs due to the addition of Al3+ ions at various concentrations resulted in visible color changes from green to blue under 365 nm UV light irradiation (Fig. 5c). As shown in Fig. 5c, upon titration with Al3+ ions, the PL intensity significantly changed at 469 and 492 nm (0–5.0 μM). The Benesi-Hildebrand and Ster-Volmet plots of the P469nm/P492nm ratio against the concentrations of Al3+ ions displayed a good linear relationship with the correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9826 and 0.9953), as shown in Fig. 5d. The detection limit (LOD) of the NSP-CDs/Al3+ at 469 and 492 nm emission intensity was 10.8 and 91.6 nM (Fig. 5e), based on the equation LOD = K × SD/S (Mohandoss et al., 2022, 2021a, 2021b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sequential sensing of Fe3+ by NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex
ce:section.ce:para: PL chemosensors with potential use in biological systems must achieve a highly selective response to the analyte over other competing organisms that coexist in the sample. By observing changes in the PL spectra of the NSP-CD solution after the addition of Al3+ and 10 μM of other metal ions for competitive experiments were considered. The solution of the NSP-CDs containing Al3+ ions and other competitive metal ions, except Fe3+ ions, showed a significantly quenched PL intensity (Gharami et al., 2017). As shown in Figs. S2a and S2b, with the addition of Fe3+ ions, the PL intensity at 469 nm for the NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex system dramatically decreased by a maximum of 72%, which may be ascribed to the interaction between the NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex system and Fe3+ ions. Moreover, the addition of different metal ions made the PL color of the NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex change both in daylight and when exposed to UV light at 365 nm (Fig. S2c). However, when 10 μM of Fe3+ ions were added, the bright blue emission color of the NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex disappeared. These findings indicate that the presence of all common competing metal ions, except for Fe3+ ions, has little effect on the ability of the NSP-CDs to detect Al3+ ions with extreme specificity.

PL titrations of the NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex with Fe3+ ions were investigated. In Fig. 6 a, the PL intensity gradually decreases with an increase in the Fe3+ ion concentration. When the Fe3+ ion concentration increased to 10 μM, the PL intensity of the NSP-CDs/Al3+ solution quickly decreased and was finally highly quenched (Mi et al., 2014). Notably, the dramatic quenching of the PL intensity at 469 nm reached a plateau at 10 μM of Fe3+. Fig. 6b, the stoichiometry of the NSP-CDs/Al3+ + Fe3+ complex was evaluated using Job's plot to analyze the binding stoichiometry of the NSP-CDs/Al3+ (Mohandoss et al., 2015). Additionally, Job's plot between the mole fraction and relative intensity variations at 469 nm was used to determine the stoichiometry of NSP-CDs/Al3+. When the molar fraction was 0.5, the absorbance at 469 nm achieved a maximum, indicating a 1:1 stoichiometry for the NSP-CDs/Al3+ + Fe3+ complex systems. Moreover, the PL quenching of NSP-CDs/Al3+ owing to the addition of Fe3+ ions at various concentrations resulted in visible color changes from strong blue to faint blue under 365 nm UV light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 6c. Fig. 6d presents the Stern-Volmer plot for the PL intensity change (P0/P) vs. Fe3+ ion concentrations (0–10 μM) at 469 nm for NSP-CDs/Al3+ binding with Fe3+ ions. Based on this titration profile, the LOD was calculated to be 50.7 nM using LOD = K × SD/S (Fig. 6e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of NSP-CDs interaction with metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: As a result of Al3+ chelation, NSP-CDs have greater PL because the charge transfer between surface functional groups has been altered. NSP-CDs exhibit excellent PL intensity when their surface functional groups, for instance –COOH, –SH, –NH, –NH2, and –OH have free electron transfers between their oxygen and carbon atoms (Scheme S2) (Mondal and Saha, 2019; Tang et al., 2019). The FTIR spectra of NSP-CDs/Al3+ (Fig. S3), upon adding Al3+ to NSP-CDs solution, Al3+ and –O of –COOH on NSP-CDs surface interact, activating the appearance of a new peak at 1072 and 491 cm−1 that correspond to the Al–O bonds (González-Gómez et al., 2019; Sooksin et al., 2018). NSP-CDs, the absorption peak associated with the CO bond stretching vibration moved to 1652 cm−1 from 1661 cm−1 after adding Al3+, demonstrating that Al3+ addition effects the –COOH group (Scheme S2). Additionally, Al3+ and NSP-CDs together cause energy transfer at the NSP-CDs surface, which reduces the PL intensity of the green emission peaks. The NSP-CDs also expand the carbon core-conjugated system, increasing the PL intensity of the blue emission peak with a significant stoke shift (Zhang et al., 2022). Al3+ chelated with the –COOH groups on the NSP-CD surfaces and decreased their ability to receive electrons and increased the probability of electron-hole recombination and resulted in PL enhancement (Gao et al., 2022; Li and Li, 2021).

The intrinsic self-calibration mechanism of the ratiometric PL sensor, along with its excellent sensitivity, high selectivity, reliable sensing, and great anti-interference performance outcomes, allows it to successfully overcome interference from the background and the detection environment. The PL sensor for Fe3+ detection was fabricated by mixing the green emission NSP-CDs and blue emission NSP-CDs/Al3+. NSP-CDs/Al3+, as the efficient energy transfer system based on the chelation between NSP-CDs and Al3+, emits a strong blue PL emission. The binding between both the –O and –N of NSP-CDs and the Al3+ inhibits the energy transfer of the surface functional groups on NSP-CDs. The formation of these two atoms (O and N) is caused by the addition of Fe3+, which receives the electron while the nitrogen and oxygen atoms of the NSP-CDs act as the electron donors. The Fe3+ atom tends to quench emission either due to a paramagnetic effect or because it contains a heavy atom (Chen et al., 2020). It is possible that excess Fe3+ quenched the PL emission of NSP-CDs/Al3+. As a result of the strong hard base nature of the nitrogen and oxygen atoms in NSP-CDs, it makes sense that it would also form a non-PL complex with Fe3+ (a strong hard acid) (Issa et al., 2020). Therefore, the above-mentioned possibilities may result in PL ‘turn-off’ response of NSP-CDs/Al3+ in presence of Fe3+ (Wang et al., 2014) (Scheme S3). Therefore, the detection mechanism of Fe3+ with NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex may be the synergistic effect of agglomeration-induced quenching and electron transfer. The structure of the NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex remains was affected by the addition of Fe3+ ions at various concentrations, indicating multiple complexation of NSP-CDs/Al3+ by Fe3+ ions.

The reversibility of a chemosensor is crucial for its practical applicability. Fe3+ titration was performed to determine whether the NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex species were reversible. The addition of Al3+ ions to the NSP-CDs improved photoluminescence. Turn-off photoluminescence was observed after the NSP-CDs/Al3+ combination progressively received more Fe3+ ions. These results indicate that Al3+ ions from NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex were chelated by Fe3+ ions (Fig. S4). Surprisingly, PL enhancement occurred again when Al3+ ions were added, demonstrating the reversible nature of the NSP-CD sensor for Al3+ ion sensing (Wang et al., 2018). These findings demonstrate the capability of the NSP-CD sensor to function as an Al3+ ion PL enhancement sensor in a fully aqueous solution.

Another exciting feature was that the designed chemosensor (i.e., NSP-CDs) could be used as a molecular logic gate with dual chemical inputs of Al3+ and Fe3+ ions, with emission at 469 nm as an output. This molecular logic gate function enables the creation of a secure password and performs essential functions for data security (Margulies et al., 2007). When no stimuli were present, the NSP-CDs did not exhibit any significant emission at 469 nm, resulting in output 0 (off-state). By contrast, when stimuli were present, the NSP-CDs exhibited considerable fluctuations in the PL intensity, resulting in outputs 0 (off-state) and 1 (on-state) (Liu et al., 2022). The two chemical inputs (In) were labeled as InAl3+ and InFe3+. As previously noted, the coordination of Al3+ ions to the NSP-CDs caused the formation of an emission band at 492 nm. The emission intensity at 469 nm gradually diminished upon adding Fe3+ ions. With Al3+ and Fe3+ ions as inputs, the emission peak at 492/469 nm can be explained as a molecular circuit with an INHIBIT logic function (Pandith et al., 2017), as shown in Fig. S5.

The possible application of the NSP-CD sensor in live-cell bio-imaging was evaluated. HeLa cells were used to test the cytotoxicity of NSP-CDs using the WST assay, which is a key concern for bio-imaging. Fig. 7 a shows the viability of HCT-116 cells treated with various concentrations of NSP-CDs (0–150 μg/mL) for 24 h (Bajpai et al., 2020). The survival rate was higher than 95% after treatment with 125 μg/mL NSP-CDs. It remained over 90% even at a relatively high concentration (150 μg/mL) (Belza et al., 2021), demonstrating that the NSP-CD sensor has low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility and thus can be used for bio-imaging.

Subsequently, the monitoring ability of NSP-CDs, NSP-CDs/Al3+, and NSP-CDs/Al3+ + Fe3+ complex system in living HCT-116 cells was investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 7b. HCT-116 cells were incubated with the free sensor NSP-CDs (125 μg/mL), NSP-CDs/Al3+ (Al3+; 5 μM), and NSP-CDs/Al3+ + Fe3+ (Fe3+; 10 μM) for 30 min at room temperature, and then images were taken. By contrast, green fluorescence was observed when HCT-116 cells were incubated with the NSP-CDs. Interestingly, enhanced fluorescence intensity from the intracellular area was observed in HCT-116 cells treated with NSP-CDs/Al3+ (Deorukhkar et al., 2015). After treatment with the NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex system, fluorescence measurements confirmed that HCT-116 cells with blue fluorescence were viable in the NSP-CDs/Al3+ system (Unnikrishnan et al., 2020). In a further complex experiment, it was found that HCT-116 cells displayed very low fluorescence when the cells were first incubated with NSP-CDs/Al3+ for 30 min at 37 °C and then incubated with Fe3+ ions (Fig. 7b). These results indicate that NSP-CDs may help detect Al3+and Fe3+ ions in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of spiked analytes in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of Al3+ and Fe3+ ions in environmental samples (tap and lake water) was observed to explore the viability of the sensor NSP-CDs in sample detection. To achieve this, artificial Al3+ and Fe3+-contaminated samples were produced separately using various known concentrations, and their concentrations were then determined using the suggested PL sensing approach. The results are presented in Table S1. According to the analytical findings, recoveries ranged from 83.4 to 114.0% for various water samples, and the relative error values (0.02–2.13%) were consistently less than 3% in every case (Lan et al., 2017; Sooksin et al., 2018; J. H. Wang et al., 2019). The Al3+ and Fe3+ ion concentrations measured using the NSP-CD PL sensor technique were remarkably close to those obtained by addition. Therefore, this technique can accurately detect Al3+ and Fe3+ ions in real water samples.

We report a simple, economic, and green method to synthesize green PL NSP-doped CDs using a one-pot hydrothermal with glutathione and H3PO4 as precursors. The obtained NSP-CDs exhibited excellent stability under various optimizations, such as pH, salt ionic strength, various solvents, UV irradiation, and high quantum yields, up to 26.7%. The average size of the NSP-CDs was 5.2 nm, exhibits an excellent water solubility with excitation-dependent green emission. Moreover, N, S, and P were successfully doped into the CDs, which was confirmed by various characterization techniques, including FTIR, XRD, TEM, and XPS. The chemosensor NSP-CDs showed high selectivity and sensitivity to Al3+ ions, with turn-on photoluminescence and a significant blue shift with blue emission. Subsequently, the enhanced photoluminescence of the NSP-CDs/Al3+ complex system could detect Fe3+ ions in the aqueous solution using a displacement strategy with high reversibility. The calculated detection limits were as low as 10.8 nM for Al3+ and 50.7 nM for Fe3+, respectively. In addition, chemosensor NSP-CDs have been successfully applied in molecular INHIBIT logic gate construction. Furthermore, fluorescence imaging of Al3+ and Fe3+ ions in living cells was successfully performed, indicating that the NSP-CD chemosensor could be used in biomedical applications. Finally, the NSP-CD chemosensor was successfully employed to simultaneously determine trace amounts of Al3+ and Fe3+ in real water samples.

Sonaimuthu Mohandoss: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Sivarasan Ganesan: Validation & Review editing. Subramanian Palanisamy: Methodology, Investigation.; SangGuan You: Methodology, Investigation. K. Velsankar: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Sakkarapani Sudhahar: Methodology, Investigation. Huang-Mu Lo: Validation & Review editing. Yong Rok Lee: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.
